title: Anime gags and Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: Anime gags and Evangelion             Quote Post by ehh123 » Wed Apr 28, 2010 10:48 am Some episodes of NGE had a lot of anime visual gags. I mean stuff like face faults, sweat drops, swirly eyes, etc. I guess it worked when it aired for the first time and people did not know the dark turn it would take, but now that everyone knows what Evangelion is all about, it seems out of place. I'm surprised that in all the threads, there isn't a discussion on how a hologram of Asuka was able to physically hit Shinji at the end of episode 9. Don't get me wrong. I love that kind of humor. It's the equivalent of Tex Avery for me. In fact, visual gags like that are one of the reasons I got into anime in the first place. I just think that for an anime like this, they sometimes overdid it. The Rebuild movies have thankfully toned it down. How do you feel about the visual gags that are in Evangelion? Do you have any favorites? Do you find them distracting?

[]:             Quote Post by toe mash » Wed Apr 28, 2010 10:56 am They are pretty annoying, but thankfully after episode 13 or so and the series takes a darker turn, like you mentioned, they almost completely stop with the visual gags. Only one I recall is in episode 17; Asuka asks Hikari what she finds in Toji, she replies something like "his compassion" and Asuka has the big sweat drop. Ofcourse many cliches like those were used in the "alternate universe" scene in ep. 26, but thats pretty special.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Apr 28, 2010 11:25 am I don't find that it bothers me, as I feel it's only the filmmaker thoroughly exploring whatever techniques to fit the stylistic storytelling of the particular moment in the series. Just like Anno holds to a more obvious "darker" form of storytelling in the latter half of the series to contrast from the lighter tone in the first half, he uses a more obvious "lighter" form of storytelling in the first half to contrast it with the darker second half. Especially upon first viewing, this sets the audience up for the rug to be pulled from under their feet. People think the styles are going to be one way... then BAM! Something unexpectedly dark happens and the audience is left scrambling to figure out what happens next. ("Wait, I thought I had this show pretty well figured out...") So because the first half is "lighter", it helps the second half be "darker" ("more tragic" would be a better way to describe it). Just like how one can't appreciate the day without the night, or can't appreciate food if they're never hungry. And that over-all technique really doesn't bother me that much. As a matter of fact, it just makes me like it more.

[]:             Quote Post by Peewit » Wed Apr 28, 2010 3:22 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever had essentially summed up everything I was going to say. The way that Eva lulls you into a false sense of security and then, suddenly, catches you with your pants down on your first time viewing is one of the reasons that I love it so much. I don't think that it would have been nearly as effective without the jokes. At first, Eva essentially disguises itself as a more general anime.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Wed Apr 28, 2010 3:52 pm Evangelion always has a nice way to take a shit on people's expectations. Honestly, I can't help but think this slow subversion of common anime visuals is amazing. Plus 1 to what Freaky Film Fan said as well.

[]:             Quote Post by ehh123 » Wed Apr 28, 2010 4:46 pm I see everyone's point. It does lead you into a false sense of security. I'm just imagining this situation. Bob: Have you considered watching Evangelion? Joe: After hearing you bragging on how deep it is, I just might. Bob: I have the DVDs. Joe: OK. I will watch it as soon as I can. (After watching 6 episodes) Bob: How is it? Joe: So far, pretty good. One thing though. Bob: What's that? Joe: You failed to mention Pen-Pen. Bob: ...Yeah. Get used to that for the next few episodes.

[]:             Quote Post by Zephros » Wed Apr 28, 2010 4:48 pm You guys basically hit it on the nose. Though I think there were times it was inappropriate. Take Misato driving along, with the END OF THE FUCKING WORLD hanging in the balance, and she's worried about her car. That was one instance of tone conflict in a specific setting. The sweatdrop scene I think was another, since that entire episode was much more serious in tone.

[]:             Quote Post by The Triforce Fighter » Wed Apr 28, 2010 5:22 pm ehh123 wrote: Joe: You failed to mention Pen-Pen. Bob: ...Yeah. Get used to that for the next few episodes.

[]:             Quote Post by Peewit » Wed Apr 28, 2010 5:27 pm The Triforce Fighter wrote: I never really saw anime as something that could tell such a good story bar your average shounen (Mainly because I grew up watching Dragonball and not much else X D) . So I thank the light hearted-ness helped ease me in. Then by the time it went all dark I could hardly leave as I grown to love the characters, plus it helps I love a good deep complex story that ends up confusing me and leaving me with un answered questions so I am compelled to watch it over and over.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Wed Apr 28, 2010 6:39 pm It's not like the eye pops or sweat drops are "canon" or anything. They're just stylization. Taking advantage of the medium and all that. Though I was thinking just yesterday how weird it'd be if End of Evangelion included the facial distortions or sweat drops you find elsewhere in the series.

[]:             Quote Post by ehh123 » Wed Apr 28, 2010 7:36 pm NAveryW wrote: It's not like the eye pops or sweat drops are "canon" or anything. They're just stylization. Taking advantage of the medium and all that.

[]:             Quote Post by SenorSquiid » Wed Apr 28, 2010 7:48 pm All the gags in Both of You Dance Like You Want to Win I thought were funny and well executed and fit the tone of the episode. But yeah, the rest of the time not so much. Except of course PenPen, who is always appropriate.

[]:             Quote Post by Synapsid » Wed Apr 28, 2010 8:28 pm NAveryW wrote: Though I was thinking just yesterday how weird it'd be if End of Evangelion included the facial distortions or sweat drops you find elsewhere in the series.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Apr 28, 2010 9:48 pm In general, NGE is much lighter on those stylized "anime-isms" than the majority of series out there. I think the scarcity of them is quite noticeable as well. I mean, Anno pretty much made an entire series out of those anime-isms in Kare Kanno, and I think their absence in NGE says a lot about the more realistic tone he was going for in terms of characters and emotions. The anime-isms that are there are much more underplayed than typical and mostly just reside in the exaggerated comedic moments.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Wed Apr 28, 2010 10:19 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: In general, NGE is much lighter on those stylized "anime-isms" than the majority of series out there.

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Thu Apr 29, 2010 12:48 am I was going to say, "In b4 Jimbo posts 'It was all according to Anno's plan.' " but it looks like he beat me to it.

[]:             Quote Post by felineki » Thu Apr 29, 2010 12:01 pm The part where Pen-Pen eats Misato's cooking and the background goes all crazy always gets me.

[]:             Quote Post by planet news » Thu Apr 29, 2010 6:34 pm I didn't mind the very, very little bit of anime exaggeration at all. I think it gives the whole show much more of a "Blue Velvet" feel, where idyllic settings are contrasted with really dark realities. Kano-faces express the mood of an idyllic "light" anime episode, which makes the journey into darkness all the more strange and intriguing. Higurashi is kind of the same. It throws in a ton of traditional anime humor that is actually enjoyable in itself, but later reveals the dark side of all of it. Makes it more interesting and powerful than just a straight-up horror/murder series.

[]:             Quote Post by tomrule123 » Thu Apr 29, 2010 6:45 pm Let me say one thing: never compare Tex Avery to anime... his techniques in animation is far more crazy than anime does... Now, my opinion on the Anime visual gags in Evangelion: well, it's all right when it's appropriate... Nobody expected a big turn in EVA at episode 16 (or 14), but hey, even Rebuild had some visual gags.

[]:             Quote Post by TehDonutKing » Thu Apr 29, 2010 7:45 pm The Triforce Fighter wrote: Pen Pen is the unsung hero of Evangelion.... I wouldn't be surprised if he was the one that stop the instrumentality project over Shinji. Maybe there's an outtake of a giant Pen Pen wrestling with a giant Rei..... Or maybe I just have an over active imagination X D
